mappings.rationale: Directly relevant as an introduction to the Classical world — the section covers Greek and Roman civilization from the Archaic period through the fall of the Roman Empire.
Covers Greek civilization from the Archaic through Classical periods, directly relevant to subsections B (Archaic Greece, the polis, Athens, Sparta) and the Classical period.
Relevant to the section's coverage of Greek philosophical developments including the pre-Socratics, Sophists, and 4th-century developments in philosophy.
Socrates is explicitly mentioned in the section's discussion of intellectual and political changes at Athens during the Peloponnesian War.
Relevant to the section's discussion of Greek culture in the 4th century BC including developments in philosophy, and Aristotle's role as Alexander's tutor.
The section devotes substantial coverage to Alexander III the Great's empire, his conquest of the Persian Empire, Bactria, and the Indus Valley (334-323 BC).
Directly covers subsection C on the Hellenistic Age (323-27 BC), including the successor kingdoms, Hellenistic culture, and the integration of the Greek world with Rome.
Directly covers subsection D on the rise of Rome, from the early Republic through the Punic Wars, the Gracchi, and the late Republic to Octavian's sole power.
Relevant to the section's coverage of Roman imperial expansion including the annexation of Britain under Claudius and the western provinces under the later empire.
Relevant to the section's multiple references to Greek and Roman literary culture, including Homer, Hesiod, Greek drama, Herodotus, Thucydides, Virgil, Horace, and Ovid.
Directly covers subsection A.3.a on the Etruscans and other Italic peoples, their origins, cities, maritime expansion, and eventual Roman conquest.
Covers the transformation of the Roman Empire in the 3rd-4th centuries AD, directly relevant to the section's treatment of the dominate, Constantine, and the eclipse of the Western Empire.
Herodotus is explicitly mentioned as the source for accounts of the Greco-Persian Wars, a major topic in the section's treatment of Archaic Greece.
Thucydides is explicitly mentioned as the source for accounts of the Peloponnesian War, a major topic in the Classical period subsection.
Homer is explicitly mentioned in the section's discussion of the Geometric Period (c. 900-750 BC) as defining the world of early Greece.
Relevant to the section's coverage of Mycenaean civilization, the Bronze Age Aegean, and the legends of early Greece that shaped Classical culture.
Relevant to the section's coverage of Hellenistic philosophical developments and late Republic Roman culture where Epicureanism was a major school.
Relevant to Hellenistic philosophical developments and Greco-Roman culture of the 1st and 2nd centuries AD, both covered in this section.
Relevant to the section's discussion of oratory and philosophy in the late Roman Republic and Greco-Roman cultural life.
Directly relevant to the section's extensive coverage of Sparta's dominance over the Peloponnese, its military-oriented polity, the Peloponnesian League, and the Spartan hegemony.
Relevant to the section's discussion of cultural efflorescence in Periclean Athens, including drama as a major art form of the Classical period.
Ovid is explicitly mentioned in the section's discussion of the culture of the Augustan Age alongside Livy, Virgil, and Horace.
Horace is explicitly mentioned in the section's discussion of the literary contributions of the Augustan Age.
Relevant to the section's discussion of Greek and Roman religious developments, Orphism, the cult of Dionysus, and the remnants of pagan culture under Julian and the late Empire.
Directly covers subsection A.3.b.i on the Celts, from the Hallstatt Period through La Tene, their occupation of Europe, and eventual subjugation by Rome and the Germans.
Relevant to the section's coverage of Celtic religion and social organization across Europe from the Hallstatt through La Tene periods.
Directly covers subsection E on the Roman Empire from Augustus through the 4th-century successors of Constantine, including provincial administration and imperial decline.
Relevant to the section's extensive coverage of Greek and Roman military history, from the Greco-Persian Wars through the Punic Wars and Roman imperial campaigns.
Relevant to the section's coverage of the rise and spread of Christianity under the Roman Empire, Constantine's conversion, and the establishment of Christianity as the sole state religion.
Relevant to the section's references to the beginnings of philosophy and science in the Archaic period and subsequent scientific developments in the Classical and Hellenistic ages.
The section explicitly discusses historiographic problems and documentary historical sources for understanding ancient European and Classical civilizations.
The section emphasizes archaeological sources including Pompeii and Herculaneum for understanding Roman civilization and pre-Classical European peoples.
Relevant to the section's coverage of the Roman Republic's political institutions and virtues, which deeply influenced Machiavelli's political thought.
Relevant to the section's discussion of Homer and Hesiod in the Geometric Period and Virgil in the Augustan Age, both central to the epic literary tradition.
Relevant to the section's discussion of Greek mythology, religious developments, panhellenic centres like Olympia and Delphi, and Roman myths of origins.
Cicero was a central figure in Roman political and philosophical life, directly relevant to the section's coverage of Classical Greco-Roman civilization.
